title: Personal Data or Not?
intro: Before you can protect data, Agent, you must recognize which of it is PERSONAL. The law's test is not "does it contain a name?" — it is "does it relate to an identified or IDENTIFIABLE living person?" Identifiable directly, or indirectly, by anyone reasonably likely to try. Judge the following evidence from around the village.

cards.item: Odile's ledger entry: "Bram of the North Gate — owes 3 silver."
cards.explain: Name plus a fact about him. Directly identified person + information relating to him. The classic case.

cards.item: The tavern sold 44 ales on festival night.
cards.explain: An aggregate about the tavern, relating to no individual. Unless the village has one legendary drinker — aggregates over tiny groups can flip.

cards.item: A report: "A cloaked figure with a limp visits the fountain every dawn."
cards.explain: No name — but a pattern that singles one person out. Identifiability, not naming, is the test. This is how tracking works.

cards.item: Division file: "Agent NIGHTINGALE filed three reports this month."
cards.explain: A codename is a pseudonym. Pseudonymized data is STILL personal data — the Division holds the key linking Nightingale to a person.
Pure statistic about the village. No individual is identified or identifiable from the number alone.

cards.item: A note: "The blacksmith's apprentice was seen near the West Gate."
cards.explain: No name — but the village has exactly ONE blacksmith's apprentice. In small populations, a role identifies a person. Context decides.

cards.item: The Cat's daily patrol route through the tavern.
cards.explain: The Cat is magnificent, but not a natural person. Data protection law protects living humans. (The Cat has filed an objection.)

cards.item: A scroll listing "households that displayed Apocalypse faction banners."
cards.explain: Households resolve to identifiable people — and this reveals beliefs/affiliations. Not just personal data: a SPECIAL CATEGORY needing extra care.
